'''Converbs''' are non-finite verb forms in [[Aelaki]] that function as adverbial subordinate clauses. They are divided into two classes based on how they interact with [[TAM]] (tense-aspect-mood) marking.

== Class I: Prefix Converbs ==

Class I converbs are formed with a '''prefix''' and retain full TAM inflection. The general syntax is:

<code>[PREFIX- [DAY-] (SUBJ-[[Ki_syllables|Ki]]) (INST-Ki) ROOT.ASPECT-(OBJ-Ki)-TAM]</code>

Because they keep the full TAM slot, Class I converbs can express the same range of evidentiality, mood, and tense distinctions as main verbs.

=== Day Markers ===

Day markers insert between the verbal prefix and the lexical root. They indicate the temporal setting of the converb clause:

* '''go-''' — general day marker
* '''goki-''' — specific day marker
* '''go-IMP-''' — imperative day marker

== Class II: Suffix Converbs ==

Class II converbs are formed with a '''suffix''' that replaces ("eats") the TAM slot. The suffix itself is '''day-sensitive''', meaning the choice of suffix form depends on when the event takes place.

Because Class II converbs lack a TAM slot, they cannot independently mark tense, aspect, or mood — these are inferred from context or the main clause.

== Major Subtypes ==

=== Causal Converb ===

The '''causal converb''' expresses reason or cause ("because", "since"). It belongs to one of the two major converb subtypes.

=== Conditional Converb ===

The '''conditional converb''' expresses conditionality ("if", "when"). It places '''heavy limitations''' on the allowed TAM categories.

==== TAM Restrictions ====

The conditional converb '''cannot take visual evidentiality''', which is strictly [[Realis|realis]]. Only the following moods are permitted with the conditional converb:

{| class="wikitable"
! Mood !! Semantic Force
|-
| [[Auditory]] || "someone else said" (reported)
|-
| Inferred || "I infer" (inferential)
|-
| Intentive || "I will" (prospective intention)
|-
| [[Optative]] || "I wish" (desiderative)
|-
| Deliberative || "should I?" (deliberative question)
|}

These restrictions reflect the inherently irrealis nature of conditional clauses — one cannot visually witness a hypothetical event.

[[Category:Aelaki grammar]]
